verses.text: David resolves to guard his mouth and stay silent before the wicked, but the suppressed grief burns until he must speak. Restraint has its limit. Sometimes the heart so full of trouble must at last pour itself out to God.

David resolves to guard his mouth and stay silent before the wicked, but the suppressed grief burns until he must speak. Restraint has its limit. Sometimes the heart so full of trouble must at last pour itself out to God.

David resolves to guard his mouth and stay silent before the wicked, but the suppressed grief burns until he must speak. Restraint has its limit. Sometimes the heart so full of trouble must at last pour itself out to God.
'LORD, make me to know mine end,' he prays, owning that his lifetime is 'as nothing' before God. He longs to grasp life's brevity. To measure our short days rightly is the beginning of wisdom.
'LORD, make me to know mine end,' he prays, owning that his lifetime is 'as nothing' before God. He longs to grasp life's brevity. To measure our short days rightly is the beginning of wisdom.
Man walks in a vain show, heaping up wealth he cannot keep; so, 'now, Lord, what wait I for? my hope is in thee.' Earthly things prove empty. When all else fades, the soul finds its only hope in God.
Man walks in a vain show, heaping up wealth he cannot keep; so, 'now, Lord, what wait I for? my hope is in thee.' Earthly things prove empty. When all else fades, the soul finds its only hope in God.

He asks deliverance from his transgressions and falls silent, 'because thou didst it,' owning God's hand in his trouble. He submits to the chastening. Faith bows to God even when it does not understand.

He asks deliverance from his transgressions and falls silent, 'because thou didst it,' owning God's hand in his trouble. He submits to the chastening. Faith bows to God even when it does not understand.
He begs God to lift the stroke, knowing that under correction man's beauty consumes like a moth. Sin is rebuked, and frailty exposed. God's discipline reminds us how fragile we are.
He begs God to lift the stroke, knowing that under correction man's beauty consumes like a moth. Sin is rebuked, and frailty exposed. God's discipline reminds us how fragile we are.

'Hear my prayer, O LORD,' he pleads, a 'stranger' and 'sojourner' like his fathers, asking a little respite before he dies. He owns himself a pilgrim. The believer passes through this world as a guest bound for home.
